text: The surgeon performs ligation and interposition of a modified Martius graft during the repair of a recto-vaginal fistula following a low anterior resection for rectal cancer.

Here we can see the clinical case, a sixty-three year old woman, operated for rectal cancer by low anterior resection with total mesorectal excision protected with a loop isostomy. Loop ileostomy is closed at six months after a correct anastomotic test. Twelve months later, the patient presented perineal sepsis with passage of gas and stool from vagina.

The clinical examination reveals the internal opening of the fistula on the anterior face of the anastomosis and a fistulous orifice on the posterior vaginal surface, confirmed by magnetic resonance imaging and anal ultrasound.
New loop ileostomy is performed. Three months later is performed the surgical repair.

The magnetic resonance imaging reveals a rectal tumor infiltrating concentrically into the rectal walls, while the mesorectal fat remains uninfiltrated. The sagittal and axial sections display the fistula structure between the rectum and the vagina.
The patient is positioned in the lithotomy position under regional anesthesia. A loose seton is visibly placed within the fistula tract.
A transverse incision is made in the rectovaginal space, and a Lone Star retractor system is used to provide enhanced visualization of the surgical plane.

The surgeon uses diathermy to develop the plane between the external annulus pincer and the posterior vaginal wall, ensuring meticulous hemostasis. The dissection is extended to expose the fibrotic tissue of the fistula structure from the previous anastomosis, with the muscular sleeve already surpassed and the fistula structure visible in the middle.

The surgeon maneuvers a curved probe through the fistula tract to aid in dissection. After dissecting the tract, it is encircled, and the probe is removed. The fistula tract is then ligated with absorbable sutures and divided.

The surgeon uses two taution retractors to achieve a clear dissection of the rectovaginal space, ensuring proper muscular interposition. Subsequently, the rectal side of the fistula tract is invaginated using interrupted absorbable sutures.
The surgeon ligates the vaginal side of the fistula tract using absorbable sutures. The incision over the labium majus is visibly marked.

It should be noted that Martius described the use of the bulbocavernosus muscle for the treatment of vesicovaginal fistulas, but other authors showed that the bulbocavernosus muscle itself is not necessary to incorporate it in the graft and that the use of the labial adipose tissue may be sufficient to perform the flap. This is called modified Martius graft or labial fat pad.
The surgeon dissects the labial fat while preserving the posterior blood supply from the internal pudendal artery. The contracting bulbocavernosus muscle is visible during the procedure.

The surgeon tunnels the dissected flap to the rectovaginal septum, positioning it over the two-layer fistula closure. Multiple sutures are used to secure the flap in place, preventing it from sliding.
A suction drain is placed in the graft space beneath the labial incision, followed by a primary closure using Vicryl Quick sutures.
The evolution of the patient was satisfactory and three months later we proceeded to the closure of the ileostomy. This is the appearance of the ones a few months later and at present.
